title: China's high-speed rail widens urban–rural disparities in air pollution and public health

data_scope.study_area: mainland China (urban vs. rural; eastern, central, western regions)
data_scope.exact_counts: number of firms, grid cells and treated cities not stated in public metadata
data_scope.availability: PM2.5, HSR and a compiled socioeconomic set are open; firm-level (MOF/STA) and health (China CDC) data are NDA-restricted, replicable only onsite at Peking University

core_conclusion: Rural China is no cleaner than urban China, yet high-speed rail lets urban areas cut waste-gas emission intensity, PM2.5 and cardiovascular-disease deaths far more than rural areas, widening the urban–rural gap. The mechanism is a spatial redistribution of resources toward cities (urban firms gain resources and efficiency), and the unequal effect grows over time and persists across geographical distances.
why_it_matters: Re-frames air pollution as a rural issue too; shows a celebrated infrastructure program (HSR) acts as a distributional instrument that widens spatial inequality; links transport policy to cardiovascular mortality; documents a persistent, growing gap; and generalizes as a warning for emerging economies.
why_nature: Couples space, infrastructure and human health; counterintuitive and policy-translatable finding; multi-source, hard-to-assemble evidence (firm emissions + gridded PM2.5 + grid health on a confidential national platform); and a transferable thesis for emerging economies broadly.
added_value_to_my_research: Explainable spatial ML / MGWR to attribute the HSR effect to ranked built-environment features
Healthcare-accessibility mediator (2SFCA/R2SFCA) to split the rural death gap into exposure vs. care-access
Inter-place emission-flow network to test pollution displacement vs. genuine abatement
Multi-source fusion (mobile-phone, social media, remote sensing) to reconstruct rural exposure from coarse PM2.5
Equity-vs-efficiency infrastructure-siting optimization with the urban–rural health gap as constraint
